episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Working Lands & Industry
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Working Lands & Industry.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [TechRadar] Fuelled by AI, Micron's $50 billion chip factory in Idaho will use billions of litres of water every year — but the US tech giant stays silent on where that new water will come from and [TechRadar] OpenAI's ‘Trusted Contact’ feature for ChatGPT users in crisis is a sign AI is becoming something much more personal, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Can BC deploy AI-driven mineral exploration technology responsibly, or must governance capacity be built before the technology accelerates discovery? Topics include: AI mineral exploration technology and KoBold Metals' competitive advantages, Institutional capacity gaps in provincial and Indigenous consultation and assessment processes, Data sovereignty and public value capture from publicly-funded geological data, Nation-level technical capacity building as a structural intervention in resource governance, Sequencing of technology deployment versus governance system readiness.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Williams Lake Food Policy Council

discussion.central_question: Can BC deploy AI-driven mineral exploration technology responsibly, or must governance capacity be built before the technology accelerates discovery?
discussion.riley_position: AI exploration technology is already operational and necessary for BC to develop critical minerals for the clean energy transition; the solution is to build institutional capacity in parallel with technology deployment, not to suppress the technology while waiting for capacity.
riley_key_evidence: KoBold Metals' Zambia copper discovery identified a target human analysts had missed by assembling disparate data sources
Tahltan Nation's established environmental monitoring and consultation protocols demonstrate that Nations can develop faster processes than provincial ones
BC wood products funding announcement shows the province already invests in industry-specific data and market development
discussion.casey_position: The pace of AI-driven discovery will overwhelm existing governance, consultation, and environmental assessment systems that weren't designed for rapid acceleration; capacity-building should precede or run in genuine parallel with deployment, not follow it.
casey_key_evidence: BC's Ministry of Mines is chronically understaffed and First Nations consultation offices are already overwhelmed with the current project queue
Tahltan's capacity took twenty-plus years of sustained political organization and cannot be copy-pasted to Nations dealing with underfunded band administration and treaty backlogs
Building required expertise at BC Geological Survey requires 2-3 senior positions that have gone unfilled for five years in the current fiscal environment
discussion.resolution: Riley and Casey converged on supporting Nation-level technical capacity building as the priority intervention—if Nations develop their own AI prospecting capability, they shift from subjects of the process to users with analytical control. They disagreed on enforcement feasibility but agreed the governance question is urgent and the window to act before exploration claims accelerate is narrow.
topics_covered: AI mineral exploration technology and KoBold Metals' competitive advantages
Institutional capacity gaps in provincial and Indigenous consultation and assessment processes
Data sovereignty and public value capture from publicly-funded geological data
Nation-level technical capacity building as a structural intervention in resource governance
Sequencing of technology deployment versus governance system readiness
